Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a study routine. I'd tell myself I'd study later, and then later never really came. Sometimes I'd flip through notes while watching TV or scroll through course materials on my phone without really absorbing anything. With 20 online courses, everything just blurred together and I couldn't keep track of what needed attention. Most weeks I'd maybe put in some hours here and there, but it was never organized or planned out.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, mostly driven by panic when I realized something was due soon. I never had set study times because my motivation just wasn't there to stick to a schedule. Some weeks I'd do nothing, then suddenly cram when deadlines loomed. Between my extracurriculars and just feeling generally bored with everything, regular study times felt impossible to maintain.
Q3: Usually I only logged in when I got an email reminder about something due or when I vaguely remembered an assignment existed. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything major, which I usually had. It was never because I was excited to learn or engage with the material. The login was more like checking a box to ease my guilt than actually wanting to be there.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became a habit for me. I'd go weeks without logging in and honestly forget it existed. Other parts of my life, like my extracurricular activities, had more structure and felt more engaging. The LMS just felt like this chore sitting in the background that I kept putting off.
Q5: When I did engage, the videos were probably the most useful because I'm a visual learner and could at least passively watch them. Sometimes I'd have them playing while doing other things, which helped me absorb a little bit. The visual diagrams and charts in some materials also stuck with me better than walls of text. But honestly, even the useful stuff didn't motivate me much.
Q6: I ignored the discussion forums almost entirely and barely touched the readings. The discussions felt pointless to me, like I was just performing engagement rather than actually learning anything. The dense text-based resources were hard to focus on when I was already bored and unmotivated. I'd open them, skim for a few seconds, then close the tab and do something else.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was quick and had an obvious payoff, like directly helping with an assignment that was due. If something felt too long or abstract, I'd lose interest immediately. I needed to see the point right away or I just couldn't bring myself to care. Visual content that I could absorb quickly without too much effort felt more worthwhile than anything requiring deep concentration.
Q8: Optional activities might as well have not existed for me. If something wasn't required, there was zero chance I was doing it. Even required activities felt like a struggle, so adding optional ones on top was just not happening. The required stuff at least had deadlines that could sometimes push me into action, but optional things had no urgency at all.
Q9: Yeah, sometimes I'd download PDFs or save materials to look at later, though 'later' often meant never. I'd occasionally flip through notes I'd made or use my phone to look up concepts on YouTube instead of the course materials. The external resources felt less boring sometimes, even if they covered the same stuff. My studying, when it happened, wasn't always connected to the LMS.
Q10: There were times I'd be thinking about course concepts or reading downloaded notes on my phone during commutes, but none of that showed up anywhere. I'd sometimes discuss topics casually with friends who were in similar courses, which helped me understand things better. The LMS wouldn't know I spent an hour watching YouTube videos explaining the same material in a more engaging way. My actual learning was disconnected from my logged activity.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download a PDF or save a video link, then never go back to the LMS for that material. I preferred having things offline where I could access them without dealing with the platform. Sometimes I'd screenshot important slides and review those instead of rewatching entire lectures. The one-time access didn't reflect how many times I might have looked at something saved on my phone.
Q12: Pretty much the whole semester felt hard to engage with, but it got worse when assignments piled up and I felt too far behind to catch up. The boredom was constant, and when stress built up, I'd just shut down instead of pushing through. There were weeks where I couldn't bring myself to open anything course-related because it all felt overwhelming and pointless. My low resources also meant I was dealing with other life pressures that made school feel secondary.
Q13: Absolutely. Being stretched thin financially and having other responsibilities made the LMS feel like just another demand I couldn't meet. When I was tired or stressed, logging in felt like too much effort for too little reward. The confusion from having so many courses made it hard to even know where to start. I'd often choose to zone out or do something less demanding instead of facing the coursework.
Q14: All the time. I'd open videos and let them play while I scrolled my phone or did other things. Sometimes I'd click through materials just to make it look like I was doing something, but my brain was completely elsewhere. There were moments I'd read the same paragraph multiple times without absorbing a single word. Being physically present in the LMS didn't mean I was mentally engaged at all.
Q15: Constantly. I knew assignments were important but the thought of starting them made me feel heavy and tired. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow became next week. The boredom made everything feel like a mountain to climb, so I'd avoid it and do literally anything else. Even when anxiety about failing crept in, it wasn't enough to overcome the avoidance.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to do anything. Without them, I would have done absolutely nothing. Even with deadlines, I'd often wait until the last possible moment and then scramble to submit something incomplete. The panic of a deadline approaching was the only motivation I could find, but it wasn't sustainable and I missed plenty anyway.
Q17: I didn't really pay attention to feedback much. By the time feedback came, I'd already mentally moved on or was too stressed about the next thing. Sometimes I'd glance at comments but not really internalize them or apply them to future work. Feedback felt like it came too late to matter, and my low motivation meant I didn't have the energy to use it constructively.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with so much content spread across so many modules. I couldn't figure out what was actually important versus what was just extra. The lack of clear priorities made me disengage because everything felt equally demanding and I couldn't handle all of it. A simpler, more guided structure might have helped me stay on track better.
Q19: The sheer volume of content made engagement harder. When I logged in and saw walls of materials and multiple assignments, I'd feel paralyzed and log right back out. Having everything online without much human interaction also made it easy to disconnect and drift away. If there had been more visual, bite-sized content or clearer pathways, maybe I would have engaged more.
Q20: I think some students might be clicking around trying to figure things out or looking for something specific without actually submitting work. Maybe they're overwhelmed and exploring without completing anything. Or they could be like me, opening things to feel productive without actually doing the work. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That kind of makes sense to me. Someone could be studying effectively offline or using other resources and just not showing up much in the LMS. They might be efficient learners who don't need to click around a lot to understand the material. My experience was more the opposite though, low activity and low performance because I just wasn't engaged anywhere.
Q22: Course logs can show when someone logged in and what they clicked on, which gives a surface picture of activity. But they miss whether the person was actually paying attention or understanding anything. They can't capture offline studying, mental engagement, or the reasons behind someone's behavior. A log might show I watched a video but not that I was scrolling Instagram the whole time.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and wanting to understand it, not just going through the motions. It's when you're mentally present and curious, not just clicking buttons to meet requirements. For me, engagement would mean feeling interested and motivated, which I honestly didn't feel this semester. True engagement is active, not passive or forced.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how bored and disconnected I felt the entire time. It can't capture that I was dealing with financial stress and other life stuff that made school feel impossible. My mental state, the heaviness of trying to care about something that felt meaningless, none of that shows up in data. The numbers just show I failed, not why or how hard it was to even try.
